---
title: "Key technologies used to build the plant package"
description: "The Rcpp/R6, Git, and continuous-integration stack behind plant — how the package gets to be both fast and flexible. Reposted from the 2016 MEE Methods blog."
date: 2016-02-23
author: "Rich FitzJohn & Daniel Falster"
categories: [announcement, engineering]
reading-time: true
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Originally published on the
[*Methods in Ecology and Evolution* Methods blog](https://methodsblog.com/2016/02/23/plant/)
on 23 February 2016, by **Rich FitzJohn and Daniel Falster**, and reposted
here in the Overstorey notebook. It accompanies the package's methods
paper, Falster *et al.*,
[doi:10.1111/2041-210X.12525](https://doi.org/10.1111/2041-210X.12525).
:::

## In brief

`plant` is an individual-based model of plant growth and competition for
light. Building it meant resolving a tension that shows up in a lot of
scientific software: the model needs to be **fast** — many individuals,
integrated through a shared light environment, over long time horizons —
while staying **flexible** enough to explore as a researcher, swapping
strategies and parameters without recompiling your understanding each
time. A few technology choices made that possible:

- **C++ via [Rcpp](https://www.rcpp.org/)** — the numerical core (the
  inner growth/assimilation loops) lives in C++ for speed, exposed to R so
  the model is driven, inspected, and plotted from an interactive session.
- **[R6](https://r6.r-lib.org/) reference classes** — a mutable,
  object-oriented API on the R side that mirrors the C++ objects, so a
  strategy or a patch behaves like a thing you hold and poke, not a bag of
  function arguments.
- **Git & GitHub** — version control as the backbone of both development
  and reproducibility: every result traces to a commit.
- **Automated testing / continuous integration** — a test suite run on
  every push, so refactors to the fast C++ core don't silently change the
  science.

These are the same foundations the rest of this site is built to respect —
results pinned to a recorded `plant` state, frozen and reproducible.

<!-- ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
     FULL REPOST BODY GOES HERE.
     Daniel: paste the original post text below (you co-authored it, so
     you can repost it here). I left the section above as an original-words
     summary; replace or keep it as you prefer. Once the body is in,
     `quarto render` and commit — no version pin needed (no code runs).
     ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
Read the original in full on the
[MEE Methods blog →](https://methodsblog.com/2016/02/23/plant/)
:::
